items.q: What is the difference between weather and climate?
items.correct: Weather is short-term atmospheric conditions at a specific time and place; climate is the long-term (typically 30-year) average of weather patterns in a region.
items.misconception: Weather happens outside and climate happens globally.
items.random_wrong: Weather refers to rain and climate refers to temperature.
items.why: Students treat them as different phenomena rather than different timescales. The key distinction is time — climate is statistical, weather is instantaneous.

items.q: Why do TNCs locate production in LICs?
items.correct: Lower labour costs, weaker regulation, access to raw materials, large potential markets, and government incentives like tax breaks in special economic zones.
items.misconception: Because TNCs want to develop poorer countries and lift them out of poverty.
items.random_wrong: Because LICs have better infrastructure than HICs.
items.why: Students attribute moral motives. TNCs are profit-driven; any development benefit is a side effect, not a goal — a critical distinction for evaluation questions.

items.q: What is the difference between a choropleth and an isoline map?
items.correct: Choropleths use coloured/shaded areas to show data values per region (e.g. population density by county); isoline maps use lines connecting points of equal value (e.g. contours, isobars).
items.misconception: Choropleth and isoline maps are the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Choropleth maps are for showing churches and isoline maps are for showing roads.
items.why: They suit different data types: choropleth for area-based discrete data; isoline for continuous variables. Choosing the wrong one misrepresents the data — a common A-level skills question.

items.q: What is the main driver of Amazon deforestation?
items.why: It's Cattle ranching. “Mining” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's Emergent. “Canopy” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Roughly what percentage of the Amazon has been lost since 1970?
items.why: Exact answer: 17 %. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
